topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope lay on my desk, stark white against the chipped laminate. Inside, the address: Austin, Texas. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow thing in my chest. I didn't move for a full minute, just stared at the ink, willing it to dissolve. Best friend. Moving across the country. It felt like a physical blow, knocking the air from my lungs. I picked up my phone, the glass cold against my palm, and scrolled through pictures of old shared moments—laughing until we cried, sneaking out, planning futures that now seemed impossibly distant. A slow, burning heat started low in my stomach, tightening my jaw until my teeth ached. I didn't cry. Instead, I slammed the phone down, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I stood up, walked to the window, and watched the familiar street below, feeling a strange, hard resolve settle over me. This wasn't the end of anything. It was just a change of scenery for them. I turned back to the desk, picked up a pen, and started aggressively underlining the address, claiming it wasn't real.

[story 2]

"Seriously?" The word scraped out of my throat, sounding brittle and unfamiliar. I watched Maya pace the living room, her shoulders hunched, the movement jerky and tight. The news had hit me like a sudden drop in temperature. Moving to California. It was the kind of grand, sweeping statement that always made me want to shout, to shatter something just to feel something real again. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, feeling the tension coil deep within my muscles. I didn't offer comfort. I just stared at her, my expression carefully blank, a mask of indifference. "So, what's the plan?" I asked, my voice flat, deliberately devoid of any warmth. I watched her flinch, the suddenness of my tone clearly registering. I took a slow, deliberate sip of water, letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable. I felt a surge of something hot and sharp—a need to prove that her departure wouldn't derail my own trajectory. I needed to show her that her leaving was just another small inconvenience in a much larger, more important game.

[story 3]

The coffee was too bitter this morning, and so was my composure. I found the text message tucked beneath a stack of bills—a confirmation email for a lease signing in San Diego. My hands clenched into fists, the knuckles turning white. I reread the message three times, each time the reality of it sinking deeper into my core. I didn't want to crumble. I didn't want to let the sadness flood out; that felt weak. Instead, I paced the small kitchen, the hardwood floor protesting under my quick, sharp steps. I stopped by the counter, leaned my forehead against the cool granite, and took a deep, ragged breath. It wasn't fair, the sudden erasure of our shared geography. But fairness didn't dictate my reaction. I straightened up, pushing off the counter with a forceful movement. I walked to the door, not to run, but to stand there, shoulders back, letting the quiet resentment simmer beneath my skin. I would handle this. I would manage the fallout. I wouldn't let this change the shape of who I was.
